ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry puff entry (FOODON_03302571); no specific green bean variant exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Green Bean Curry Puff piece as sold by Old Chang Kee weighs approximately 65 g and is typically consumed as a single snack serving.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Green Bean) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists a generic green bean curry puff entry. Values are taken from the HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food database for 'Curry Puff, Green Bean' and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy mung bean and fried pastry data to validate plausibility. Per-100g values are derived from the HPB per-piece data normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for green bean curry puff. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous fried wheat-flour pastry with sweet legume filling. Mung bean paste has a reported GI of approximately 31–42 (low), while fried wheat pastry dough has a GI of approximately 55–65. The composite GI of 55 reflects the dominant wheat-flour pastry shell contribution, consistent with analogous filled fried pastry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 26.1) / 100 ≈ 14.4, where carbohydrate per 65 g serving = 40.2 × 0.65 = 26.1 g.

hypertension.guidance: Each 65 g serving provides approximately 182 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one piece and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods to stay within the recommended 2000 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 14 per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption to one piece per occasion and pair with a low-GI food or protein source to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and trans fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contributes approximately 4 g saturated fat and a small amount of trans fat from deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict intake to occasional consumption and choose baked alternatives where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Bean Curry Puff (绿豆咖喱角; Karipap Kacang Hijau) is a Singaporean fried pastry snack consisting of a flaky, deep-fried wheat-flour shell encasing a sweetened mung bean (green bean) paste filling. It is a signature product of Old Chang Kee, a well-known Singaporean snack chain, and is sold under the branded name 'Green Bean Feel'in'. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour, vegetable shortening, and water, crimped at the edges in the traditional karipap style, and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The filling is a smooth, lightly sweetened mung bean paste, distinct from the more common spiced potato or sardine curry puff variants. Each piece weighs approximately 65 g and provides around 202 kcal, 9.4 g fat, 26.1 g carbohydrate, and 3.8 g protein. The low glycemic index (estimated 55) and moderate glycemic load (approximately 14 per serving) make it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation. The saturated fat content from deep-frying is a consideration for cardiovascular health. The snack is widely available at Old Chang Kee outlets across Singapore and is popular as a breakfast item, afternoon snack, or on-the-go bite. It is halal-certified and suitable for vegetarians. The mung bean filling provides modest dietary fibre (approximately 1.8 g per serving) and small amounts of iron and B vitamins.

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo provides a green bean curry puff entry for Singapore, which is the highest-priority regional source. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB per-piece data normalised to per-100g and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy mung bean and fried pastry data. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar fried wheat-flour pastries with legume fillings; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database for this item. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported; this is a plant-based snack with no animal-derived filling, so B12 is expected to be negligible. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per HPB source as the filling is plant-based (mung bean paste) and no egg or animal fat is listed in the standard formulation. GI is an analogous estimate at 55 (low) based on fried wheat-flour pastry with sweet legume filling from University of Sydney GI Database analogues; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. The food_name has been normalised from the branded menu name 'Green Bean Feel'in' to the canonical English name 'Green Bean Curry Puff' for database purposes; the branded name is preserved in aliases.
